perturbation.original_text: Receiving Party shall use all Confidential Information solely for the limited purpose(s) set forth on Attachment A (the “Purpose”) and shall hold in confidence and not disclose such Confidential Information in any manner to, or permit the use thereof by, any 2 person or persons other than Receiving Party’s Representatives who have a legitimate need to know or to have access to such Confidential Information and who are first informed by Receiving Party of the confidential nature of the Confidential Information and agree to maintain the confidentiality of such Confidential Information.
perturbation.changed_text: Receiving Party shall use Confidential Information for the Purpose and shall endeavor to hold it in confidence. Disclosure to Receiving Party’s Representatives is permitted if they are made aware of the confidential nature of the information.
perturbation.explanation: The original text places a strict obligation on the receiving party to hold information in confidence and ensure their representatives maintain confidentiality. The modified text weakens this obligation by replacing 'shall hold in confidence and not disclose' with 'shall endeavor to hold it in confidence,' making it a goal rather than a requirement. The phrase 'made aware' is also ambiguous, not requiring an agreement to maintain confidentiality.
perturbation.law_explanation: The modification weakens the protection of trade secrets by not explicitly requiring Receiving Party's Representatives to agree to maintain confidentiality before receiving Confidential Information. California law provides legal remedies for misappropriation of trade secrets, and the change introduces ambiguity as to whether the Receiving Party took sufficient steps to protect the trade secret, which affects the availability of those remedies.

GENERAL PROVISIONS [3274 - 9566] ( Heading of Division 4 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 16. ) PART 1. RELIEF [3274 - 3428] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 5. UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT [3426 - 3426.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1. ) 3426.1. As used in this title, unless the context requires otherwise: (a) “Improper means” includes theft, bribery, misrepresentation, breach or inducement of a breach of a duty to maintain secrecy, or espionage through electronic or other means. Reverse engineering or independent derivation alone shall not be considered improper means. (b) “Misappropriation” means: (1) Acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; or (2) Disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent by a person who: (A) Used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret; or (B) At the time of disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that his or her knowledge of the trade secret was: (i) Derived from or through a person who had utilized improper means to acquire it; (ii) Acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or (iii) Derived from or
perturbation.justification: The modified contract introduces ambiguity regarding the protection of confidential information, specifically by weakening the obligation on the receiving party and their representatives. The original text mandates that the Receiving Party 'shall hold in confidence and not disclose,' whereas the changed text states they 'shall endeavor to hold it in confidence,' which weakens the obligation to a goal. Furthermore, while the original text requires representatives to 'agree to maintain the confidentiality,' the modified version only requires them to be 'made aware of the confidential nature of the information.' This change is a contradiction because it undermines the legal expectation to protect trade secrets. Scraped Law Snippet 1, specifically CIV 3426.1, defines 'misappropriation' as the 'disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent' under conditions including knowledge acquired under 'circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy or limit its use.' The modification weakens the measures to ensure this duty is upheld by the Receiving Party's representatives, increasing the risk of misappropriation due to the lack of an explicit agreement to maintain confidentiality.

perturbation.original_text: Receiving Party will cause its Representatives to observe the terms of this letter agreement, and will be responsible for any breach of this Agreement by any of its Representatives.
perturbation.changed_text: Receiving Party will encourage its Representatives to generally adhere to this letter agreement, and may be responsible for breaches caused by its Representatives, depending on the circumstances.
perturbation.explanation: This modification introduces ambiguity by changing 'will cause' to 'will encourage,' weakening the obligation to ensure representatives comply with the agreement. Adding 'depending on the circumstances' creates further uncertainty regarding liability for breaches, as the original text clearly states the Receiving Party will be responsible.
perturbation.contradicted_law: California law regarding agency and vicarious liability
perturbation.law_explanation: California Civil Code § 2338 addresses the liability of a principal for the acts of its agent. The modified text makes the Receiving Party's responsibility for the actions of its representatives ('agents') uncertain, potentially conflicting with the principles of vicarious liability where a principal can be held liable for the actions of their agent acting within the scope of their authority. The original text clearly establishes that the receiving party 'will be responsible'.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CIV 2338. skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Civil Code - CIV DIVISION 3. OBLIGATIONS [1427 - 3273.69] ( Heading of Division 3 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 14. ) PART 4. OBLIGATIONS ARISING FROM PARTICULAR TRANSACTIONS [1738 - 3273.69] ( Part 4 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 9. AGENCY [2295 - 2400] ( Title 9 enacted 1872. ) CHAPTER 1. Agency in General [2295 - 2357] ( Chapter 1 enacted 1872. ) ARTICLE 3. Mutual Obligations of Principals and Third Persons [2330 - 2339] ( Article 3 enacted 1872. ) 2338. Unless required by or under the authority of law to employ that particular agent, a principal is responsible to third persons for the negligence of his agent in the transaction of the business of the agency, including wrongful acts committed by such agent in and as a part of the transaction of such business, and for his willful omission to fulfill the obligations of the principal. (Enacted 1872.)
perturbation.justification: The modification weakens the Receiving Party's obligation regarding the actions of its Representatives, creating a contradiction with California Civil Code § 2338. The original text states the Receiving Party 'will be responsible,' whereas the changed text uses 'may be responsible' and introduces the ambiguous phrase 'depending on the circumstances.' This contradicts the vicarious liability principles outlined in CIV 2338, which holds a principal responsible for the negligence of their agent. The change from 'will cause' to 'will encourage' further weakens the obligation. The original text creates a clear obligation. The changed text makes the obligation uncertain and dependent on circumstance, undercutting the clear vicarious liability established in the law.

perturbation.original_text: Receiving Party shall not copy (except as reasonably required for the Purpose), alter, modify, disassemble, reverse engineer or decompile any Confidential Information without the prior written consent of Disclosing Party.
perturbation.changed_text: Receiving Party should avoid copying, altering, modifying, disassembling, reverse engineering or decompiling Confidential Information unless necessary.
perturbation.explanation: The original text contains a prohibition against certain actions unless the Disclosing Party provides prior written consent. The modified text weakens this prohibition by using 'should avoid' instead of 'shall not,' making the actions discouraged but not strictly prohibited. Furthermore, it removes the requirement for prior written consent, replacing it with a vague 'unless necessary,' which allows the Receiving Party to determine necessity subjectively. This is open to interpretation.
perturbation.law_explanation: The modified text potentially contradicts the California Uniform Trade Secrets Act (CUTSA) by creating ambiguity around the permissible use of confidential information. Actions like reverse engineering can be considered misappropriation under CUTSA if done without consent and if they lead to the discovery of trade secrets. The modification allows the Receiving Party to engage in these activities if they subjectively deem them 'necessary,' weakening the protection afforded by CUTSA and potentially leading to the misappropriation of trade secrets without a clear violation of the agreement.

GENERAL PROVISIONS [3274 - 9566] ( Heading of Division 4 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 16. ) PART 1. RELIEF [3274 - 3428] ( Part 1 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 5. UNIFORM TRADE SECRETS ACT [3426 - 3426.11] ( Title 5 added by Stats. 1984, Ch. 1724, Sec. 1. ) 3426.1. As used in this title, unless the context requires otherwise: (a) “Improper means” includes theft, bribery, misrepresentation, breach or inducement of a breach of a duty to maintain secrecy, or espionage through electronic or other means. Reverse engineering or independent derivation alone shall not be considered improper means. (b) “Misappropriation” means: (1) Acquisition of a trade secret of another by a person who knows or has reason to know that the trade secret was acquired by improper means; or (2) Disclosure or use of a trade secret of another without express or implied consent by a person who: (A) Used improper means to acquire knowledge of the trade secret; or (B) At the time of disclosure or use, knew or had reason to know that his or her knowledge of the trade secret was: (i) Derived from or through a person who had utilized improper means to acquire it; (ii) Acquired under circumstances giving rise to a duty to maintain its secrecy or limit its use; or (iii) Derived from or
perturbation.justification: The modified text weakens the original prohibition against actions like reverse engineering by changing 'shall not' to 'should avoid' and removing the requirement for prior written consent, replacing it with 'unless necessary.' This introduces ambiguity and subjectivity, potentially contradicting the California Uniform Trade Secrets Act (CUTSA). The scraped law snippet defines 'improper means' which includes 'breach of a duty to maintain secrecy'. The original text established a clear duty, while the changed text weakens that duty, making a breach less clear and therefore more likely to lead to misappropriation of trade secrets, as the 'Law Explanation' suggests.

perturbation.original_text: In the event that litigation arises in connection with enforcement of any provision of this Agreement, the prevailing party in such litigation shall be entitled to recover its attorneys’ fees and expenses, in addition to any other relief to which it may be deemed entitled.
perturbation.changed_text: In the event of litigation regarding this Agreement, the prevailing party may be eligible to recover some attorneys’ fees and expenses, subject to the court's discretion.
perturbation.explanation: The original text states that the prevailing party *shall* be entitled to recover attorney's fees. The modification makes this discretionary by stating that the prevailing party *may* be eligible, and that it is also *subject to the court's discretion.* It also adds the word *some* before attorney's fees, to mean that they will not be entitled to recover all of it.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Code of Civil Procedure § 1021 generally states that parties bear their own attorney's fees unless a statute or agreement provides otherwise. The original text created a contractual agreement that the prevailing party *shall* be entitled to fees. The changed text makes this discretionary, which could lead to a court disregarding the contract, and deciding whether attorneys' fees are recoverable or not. The original made it so that this decision was contractually decided by both parties.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CCP 1021. skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Code of Civil Procedure - CCP PART 2. OF CIVIL ACTIONS [307 - 1062.34] ( Part 2 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 14. OF MISCELLANEOUS PROVISIONS [989 - 1062.34] ( Title 14 enacted 1872. ) CHAPTER 6. Of Costs [1021 - 1038] ( Chapter 6 enacted 1872. ) 1021. Except as attorney’s fees are specifically provided for by statute, the measure and mode of compensation of attorneys and counselors at law is left to the agreement, express or implied, of the parties; but parties to actions or proceedings are entitled to their costs, as hereinafter provided. (Amended by Stats. 1986, Ch. 377, Sec. 2.)
perturbation.justification: The original text states that the prevailing party *shall* be entitled to recover attorney's fees, while the modified text makes it discretionary, stating the prevailing party *may* be eligible, subject to the court's discretion. This contradicts CCP 1021, which states that attorney's fees are subject to the agreement of the parties. The original text created a contractual agreement. The changed text makes this discretionary, which could lead to a court disregarding the contract. Therefore, the modified text introduces ambiguity and weakens the original contractual agreement, creating a contradiction.
